author: John Chrysostom
book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.

ccel.title: NPNF1-09. St. Chrysostom: On the Priesthood; Ascetic Treatises; Select Homilies and Letters; Homilies on the Statutes

title: Homily IX

source.title: NPNF1-09. St. Chrysostom: On the Priesthood; Ascetic Treatises; Select Homilies and Letters; Homilies on the Statutes

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Commendation of those who had laid aside the practice of swearing.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: It is shown that no one need scruple about hearing the divine oracles in the Church after a meal.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Answer to the question, Why it was so long before the Holy Scriptures were given?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Comment on the passage, “The heavens declare the glory of God,” with a description of the natural world.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And finally, an admonition against swearing.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: It was but lately that I spoke to you as I do now to you again!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And O that I could be always with you,—yea, rather am I always with you, though not by bodily presence, yet by the power of love!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For I have no other life but you, and the care of your salvation.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: As the husbandman hath no other anxiety, but about his seeds and his harvests; and the pilot about the waves and the harbours; so the preacher is anxious with respect to his auditors and their progress, even as I am at the present time!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Wherefore I bear you all upon my mind, not only here, but also at home.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For if the multitude be great, and the measure of my heart be narrow, yet love is wide; and “ye are not straitened in us.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: I will not add what follows next, for neither are we straitened with you.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Whence is this apparent?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Because I have met with many who have said, “We have performed the precept, by making rules for each other, defining penalties for those who swear, and enforcing punishment upon those who transgress this law.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: A punishment which is indeed well becoming you, and which is a sign of the greatest charity.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For I am not ashamed of making myself busy in these matters, since this love of interference does not proceed from idle curiosity but from tender care.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For if it be no reproach to the physician to make enquiry concerning the patient, neither is it any fault in us to be ever asking about your salvation; since thus being informed what has been accomplished, and what has been left undone, we shall be able to apply the further remedies with the requisite knowledge.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: These things we have ascertained by enquiry; and we give thanks to God that we have not sown our seed upon rocks, nor dropped it amidst thorns; and that we have neither needed much time, nor long delay, in order that we might reap the harvest.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: On this account I have you continually upon my heart.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: On this account I do not feel the labours of teaching, being eased of the burden by the profit of the hearer.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: This reward is, indeed, sufficient to recruit our strength, to give us wings, to elevate us, and to persuade us to undergo the utmost toil on your behalf.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Since therefore ye have manifested much generosity of feeling, suffer us to discharge the further debt of which we gave a promise the other day; although indeed I see not all present who were here when I made the promise.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: What, I would ask, can be the cause of this?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: What hath repelled them from our table?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: He that hath partaken of a bodily meal, it would seem, has thought it an indignity after receiving material food, to come to the hearing of the divine oracles.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But not rightly do they think thus.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For if this were improper, Christ would not have gone through His large and long discourses after that mystic supper; and if this had been unsuitable, He would not, when He had fed the multitude in the desert, have communicated His discourses to them after that meal.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For (if one must say something startling on this point), the hearing of the divine oracles at that time is especially profitable.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For when thou hast made up thy mind that after eating and drinking thou must repair also to the assembly, thou wilt assuredly be careful, though perchance with reluctance, of the duty of sobriety; and wilt neither be led away at any time into excess of wine, or gluttony.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For the thought, and the expectation of entering the church, schools thee to partake of food and drink with becoming decency; lest, after thou hast entered there, and joined thy brethren, thou shouldest appear ridiculous to all present, by smelling of wine, and unmannerly eructation.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: These things I now speak not to you who are now present, but to the absent; that they may learn them through your means.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For it is not having eaten that hinders one’s hearing, but listlessness.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But thou whilst deeming it to be a condemnation not to fast, then addest another fault, which is far greater and heavier, in not being a partaker of this sacred food; and having nourished the body, thou consumest the soul with famine.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Yet what kind of apology hast thou for doing this?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For in the matter of fasting thou hast, perhaps, bodily weakness to plead, but what hast thou to say with respect to hearing?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For surely weakness of body is no impediment to thy partaking of the divine oracles!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: If I had said, “Let no one who has breakfasted mix with us;” “let no one who has eaten be a hearer,” thou wouldest have had some kind of excuse; but now, when we would fain drag, entice, and beseech you to come, what apology can ye have for turning away from us?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: The unfit hearer is not he that hath eaten and drunk; but he who gives no heed to what is said, who yawns, and is slack in attention, having his body here, but his mind wandering elsewhere, and such a one, though he may be fasting, is an unprofitable hearer.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: On the other hand, the man who is in earnest, who is watchful and keeps his mind in a state of attention, though he may have eaten and drunk, will be our most suitable hearer of all.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For this rule, indeed, very properly prevails with relation to the secular tribunals and councils.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Inasmuch as they know not how to be spiritually wise, therefore they eat not to nourishment, but to bursting; and they drink often to excess.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For this reason, as they render themselves unfit for the management of their affairs, they shut up the court-houses and council-chambers in the evening and at midday.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But here there is nothing of this sort,—God forbid!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But he who has eaten will rival him who fasts, as far as regards sobriety of soul; for he eats and drinks, not so as to distend the stomach, or to darken the reason, but in such a way as to recruit the strength of the body when it has become weakened.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But enough of this admonition.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: It is time now to deal with our subject; although our mind holds back and shrinks from giving this instruction, on account of those who are not come.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And just as an affectionate mother when she is about to spread out her table, grieves and laments when all her children are not there, thus also do I now suffer; and when I think of the absence of our brethren, I am reluctant to discharge my debt.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But ye have it in your power to rid me of this tardiness.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For if ye promise me that ye will convey to them an exact report of all I say, we shall readily pay you down the whole; for thus the instructions, charitably afforded on your part, will make up to them for their absence; and ye will hear me the more attentively, knowing that you must necessarily give an account of these things to others.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: In order then that our subject may be made the clearer, let us take it up and repeat it from the beginning.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: We were enquiring, then, the other day, “On what account the Scriptures were delivered after so many years.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For this Book was delivered neither in the time of Adam, nor of Noah, nor of Abraham, but in that of Moses.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And I hear many who say, that if the Book was profitable, it ought to have been delivered from the very beginning; but if it was useless, it ought not to have been delivered afterwards.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But this is an obsolete argument; for it is not quite true that anything which is profitable ought to have been delivered from the beginning, nor if anything was delivered from the beginning, is it quite necessary that the same should continue afterwards.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For example; Milk is useful, yet it is not always given; but it is given to us only when we are children; and solid food is useful; but no one ever gives it us in the beginning of our life, but when we have passed out of the age of childhood.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Again, the summer season is useful; but it does not show itself constantly; and the winter season is advantageous; yet this too makes room for others.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Do they say that the Scriptures are not useful?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: I reply; they are most useful and most necessary.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And if so useful, for what reason then, say they, were they not delivered to us from the beginning?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: It was because God was desirous of instructing the nature of man, not by letters, but by things.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But what does the expression “by things” signify?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: By means of the Creation itself.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Observe then, how the Apostle, alighting upon this same topic, and directing himself to those very Greeks who said, that they had not from the beginning learnt the knowledge of God from the Scriptures, frames his answer.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Having said that, “the wrath of God is revealed from heaven against all ungodliness and unrighteousness of men, who hold the truth in unrighteousness;” when he saw that he was met by an objection; and that many would still enquire, from whence the Gentiles knew the truth of God, he goes on to add, “Because that which may be known of God is manifest in them.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But how is it manifest in them?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: How were they able to know God, and who hath shewed?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Declare this.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: “God,” saith he, “hath shewed it unto them.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: In what manner?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: By the sending of what kind of prophet? what evangelist? what kind of teacher? if the holy Scriptures were not yet given.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: “The invisible things of Him,” says he, “from the creation of the world are clearly seen, being understood by the things that are made, even His eternal Power and Godhead.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But what he means is just this, He hath placed His Creation in the midst, before the eyes of all men; in order that they may guess at the Creator from His works; which, indeed, another writer has referred to; “For from the greatness and beauty of the creatures, proportionably the Maker of them is seen.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Seest thou the greatness?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Marvel at the power of Him that made it!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Seest thou the beauty? be astonished at the wisdom which adorned it!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: This it was which the prophet signified when he said, “The heavens declare the glory of God.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: How then, tell me, do they declare it?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Voice they have none; mouth they possess not; no tongue is theirs! how then do they declare?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: By means of the spectacle itself.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For when thou seest the beauty, the breadth, the height, the position, the form, the stability thereof during so long a period; hearing as it were a voice, and being instructed by the spectacle, thou adorest Him who created a body so fair and strange!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: The heavens may be silent, but the sight of them emits a voice, that is louder than a trumpet’s sound; instructing us not by the ear, but through the medium of the eyes; for the latter is a sense which is more sure and more distinct than the former.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For if God had given instruction by means of books, and of letters, he who knew letters would have learnt what was written; but the illiterate man would have gone away without receiving any benefit from this source, unless some one else had introduced him to it; and the wealthy man would have purchased the Bible, but the poor man would not have been able to obtain it.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Again, he who knew the language that was expressed by the letters, might have known what was therein contained; but the Scythian, and the Barbarian, and the Indian, and the Egyptian, and all those who were excluded from that language, would have gone away without receiving any instruction.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: This however cannot be said with respect to the heavens; but the Scythian, and Barbarian, and Indian, and Egyptian, and every man that walks upon the earth, shall hear this voice; for not by means of the ears, but through the sight, it reaches our understanding.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And of the things that are seen, there is one uniform perception; and there is no difference, as is the case with respect to languages.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Upon this volume the unlearned, as well as the wise man, shall be alike able to look; the poor man as well as the rich man; and wherever any one may chance to come, there looking upwards towards the heavens, he will receive a sufficient lesson from the view of them: and the prophet himself intimated and indicated this fact, that the creation utters this voice so as to be intelligible to barbarians, and to Greeks, and to all mankind without exception, when he spoke on this wise; “There is no speech, nor language, where there voice is not heard.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: What he means is to this effect, that there is no nation or tongue which is unable to understand this language; but that such is their utterance, that it may be heard of all mankind.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And that not merely of the heavens, but of the day and night.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But how of the day and night?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: The heavens, indeed, by their beauty and magnitude, and by all the rest, astonish the beholder, and transport him to an admiration of the Creator; but as to the day and night, what can these show us of the same kind?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Nothing certainly of the same kind, but other things which are not inferior to them; as for example; the harmony, and the order which they so accurately observe.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For when thou considerest how they distribute between them the whole year, and mutually divide the length of the whole space, even as if it were by a beam and scales, thou wilt be astonished at Him who hath ordered them!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For just as certain sisters dividing their father’s inheritance among themselves with much affection, and not insulting one another in the smallest degree, even so too the day and the night distribute the year with such an equality of parts, with the utmost accuracy; and keep to their own boundaries, and never push one another aside.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Never hath the day been long in winter; and in like manner never hath the night been long in summer, whilst so many generations have passed away; but during so great an interval and length of time one hath not defrauded the other even in the smallest degree; not of half an hour’s space, no, nor of the twinkling of an eye!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Therefore also the Psalmist, struck with astonishment at the equality of this distribution, exclaimed, “Night unto night sheweth knowledge.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: If thou knowest how to meditate wisely on these matters, thou wilt admire the Being who fixed these immoveable boundaries even from the beginning.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Let the avaricious hear these things; and those who are coveting the wealth of others; and let them imitate the equality of the day and night.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Let those who are puffed up and high-minded also hear; and those who are unwilling to concede the first places to others!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: The day gives place to the night, and does not invade the territory of others!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But thou, whilst always enjoying honour, canst thou not bear to share it with thy brethren?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Consider also with me the wisdom of the Lawgiver.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: In winter He hath ordered that the night should be long; when the germs are tender, and require more coolness; and are unable to sustain the hotter rays of the sun; but when they are somewhat grown, the day again increases with them, and becomes then the longest, when the fruit has now attained ripeness.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And this is a beneficial arrangement not only for seeds, but for our bodies.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For since during winter, the sailor, and the pilot, and the traveller, and the soldier, and the farmer, sit down for the most part at home, fettered by the frost; and the season is one of idleness; God hath appointed that the greater part of this time should be consumed in night, in order that the length of the day might not be superfluous, when men were unable to do anything.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Who can describe the perfect order of the seasons; and how these, like some virgins dancing in a circle, succeed one another with the happiest harmony; and how those who are in the middle cease not to pass over to the opposite ones with a gradual and noiseless transition?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Therefore, neither are we overtaken by the summer immediately after winter; nor by the winter immediately after the summer; but mid-way the spring is interposed; that while we gently and gradually take up one season after the other, we may have our bodies hardened to encounter the summer heat without uneasiness.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For since sudden changes to opposite extremes are productive of the worst injury and disease, God hath contrived that after winter we should take up the spring, and after the spring the summer; and after the summer the autumn; and thus transport us to winter, so that these changes from seasons which are opposite, should come upon us harmlessly and by degrees, through the aid of intermediate ones.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Who then is so wretched and pitiable, that beholding the heavens; and beholding sea, and land; and beholding this exact adjustment of the seasons, and the unfailing order of day and night, he can think that these things happen of their own accord, instead of adoring Him who hath arranged them all with a corresponding wisdom!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But I have yet somewhat more to say on this head.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For not only, indeed, does the magnitude and beauty of the creation, but also the very manner of it, display a God who is the artificer of the universe.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For since we were not present at the beginning, whilst he was engaged in the work of forming and creating all things; nor had we been present, could we have known how they came into being, the power that disposed them being invisible; He hath made the mode of this creation to become our best teacher, by compounding all things in a manner which transcends the course of nature.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Perhaps what I have said, is not sufficiently clear.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Therefore it is necessary that I should again repeat it in a clearer manner.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: All men, then, must admit that it is the course of nature for water to be supported on the earth, and not the earth on the waters.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For the earth being a certain dense, hard, unyielding, and solid substance, is easily able to support the nature of water; but the water, which is fluid, and rare, and soft, and diffusive, and giving way to all it meets with, must be unable to support any solid body, though it were of the lightest kind.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Often indeed when a small pebble fails upon it, it yields, and makes way, and sends it down to the bottom.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: When therefore thou beholdest not a small pebble, but the whole earth borne upon the waters, and not submerged, admire the power of Him who wrought these marvellous things in a supernatural manner!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And whence does this appear, that the earth is borne upon the waters?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: The prophet declares this when he says, “He hath founded it upon the seas, and prepared it upon the floods.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And again: “To him who hath founded the earth upon the waters.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: What sayest thou?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: The water is not able to support a small pebble on its surface, and yet bears up the earth, great as it is; and mountains, and hills, and cities, and plants, and men, and brutes; and it is not submerged!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: What do I say?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Is not submerged?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: How comes it to pass, that since the water has been in close contact with it below, during so long a period, it has not been dissolved, and the whole of it become mud?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For the substance of wood, when soaked in water but a little time, is rotted and dissolved; and why do I say of wood?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: What can be firmer than iron? yet often this is softened, when it remains a long time in water; and well it may.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For it derives its substance from the earth.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Therefore many run-away servants, when they make their escape, dragging their shackles and chains along with them, go to brooks of water, and thrust their shackled feet therein, and after making the iron softer by this means, they easily break it by striking it with a stone.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Iron, forsooth, is softened, and wood is rotted, and stones are worn away by the nature of water; yet so great a mass as the earth hath remained such a length of time lying upon the waters, without being either submerged, or dissolved, and destroyed!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And who is there that must not feel astonished and amazed at these things; and confidently pronounce that they are not the works of nature, but of that Providence which is above nature?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Therefore one speaks thus: “Who hangeth the earth upon nothing.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And another observes, “In His hands are the corners of the earth.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And again: “He hath laid the foundation of it upon the seas.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And these declarations, though they seem contrary to one another, have yet an entire agreement.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For he that said, “He hath laid the foundation of it upon the seas,” meant the same thing as he did who declared, “He hath hung it upon nothing.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For its standing upon the waters is just the same thing as hanging upon nothing.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Where then is it suspended and placed?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Hear the same one saying, “In His hands are the corners of the earth.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Not that God hath hands, but that thou mayest know that His power it is, providing for all things which holds together and supports the body of the earth!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But if thou believest not what I now say, believe what thou beholdest! for even in another element it is possible to find this admirable workmanship.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For it is the nature of fire to tend upwards, and to be always mounting aloft; and although you force and constrain it never so much, it cannot submit to have its course directed downwards.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For often, when we are carrying a lighted torch, although we incline its head downwards, we cannot compel the force of the flame to direct itself to the ground; but still it turns upward, and passes from below toward that which is above.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But with respect to the sun, God hath made it quite the contrary.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For He hath turned his beams toward the earth, and made his light to direct itself downward, all but saying to him by the very shape (of the heavens), “Look downward.—Shine upon men, for thou wert made for them!”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: The light, indeed, of a candle cannot be made to submit to this; but this star, great and marvellous as it is, bends downward, and looks toward the earth, which is contrary to the nature of fire; owing to the power of Him who hath commanded it.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Wouldest thou have me speak of another thing of the like kind?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Waters embrace the back of the visible heaven on all parts; and yet they neither flow down, nor are moved out of their place, although the nature of water is not of this kind.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For it easily runs together into what is concave; but when the body is of a convex form, it glides away on all sides; and not even a small portion is capable of standing upon such a figure.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But, lo! this wonder is found to exist in the heavens; and the prophet, again, to intimate this very circumstance, observes, “Praise the Lord, ye waters that are above the heavens.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Besides, the water hath not quenched the sun; nor hath the sun, which hath gone on his way beneath for so long a time, dried up the water that lies above.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Dost thou desire that we should lead thee down again to the earth, and point out the marvel?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Seest thou not this sea abounding with waves, and fierce winds; yet this sea, spacious, and large, and furious as it is, is walled in with a feeble sand!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Mark also the wisdom of God, He permitted it not to be at rest, nor tranquil, lest thou shouldest suppose its good order to be of mere natural regulation; but remaining within its limits, it lifts up its voice, and is in tumult, and roars aloud, and raises its waves to a prodigious height.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But when it comes to the shores, and beholds the sand, it breaks up, and returns back again within itself; teaching thee, by both these things, that it is not the work of nature that it remains within its boundaries, but the work of Him whose power restrains it!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For this cause accordingly He hath made the wall feeble; and hath not encompassed these shores with wood, or stone, or mountains, lest thou shouldest impute the regulation of the elements to such things.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And, therefore, God Himself, upbraiding the Jews with this very circumstance, said, “Fear ye not Me, which have placed the sand for the bound of the sea that it cannot pass it.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But the marvellous thing is not this only, that He hath made a great and admirable world; and that He hath compacted it in a way above the usual course of nature; but that He hath also constituted it out of opposite things; such as hot and cold, dry and moist, fire and water, earth and air, and that these contrary elements, of which this whole universe consists, though continually at strife one with another, are not consumed of one another.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: The fire hath not overrun and burnt up all things; the water hath not overflowed and drowned the whole earth.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: With respect to our bodies, however, these effects really take place; and upon the increase of the bile, fever is generated; and the whole animal frame sustains an injury; and when there is a superabundance of phlegm, many diseases are produced which destroy the animal.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But in the case of the universe, nothing of this kind happens; but each thing remains held as it were by a kind of bridle and band; preserving, by the will of the Creator, its own boundaries; and their strife becomes a source of peace to the whole.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Are not these things evident even to a blind man? and are not even the simple easily able to comprehend, that they were made, and are upheld, by some Providence?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For who is so silly and senseless, that beholding such a mass of substances, such beauty, such combination, the continual strife of such vast elements, their opposition, and yet durability, would not reason with himself and say, “If there were not some Providence to uphold the mass of these bodies, not permitting the universe to fall to pieces, it could not remain; it could not have been lasting.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: So perfect is the order of the seasons, such the harmony of the day and night, so many the kinds of brute animals, and plants, and seeds, and herbs, that preserve their course, and yet, to the present day, none has ever fallen into decay or sudden dissolution.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: We might continue to speak not only of these things, but also of many others, which are even more profound; and might moralise even upon the Creation itself; but reserving these subjects for the morrow, let us earnestly endeavour to retain what has been said, and to convey it to the rest.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: I know indeed, that the abstruseness of these speculations has seemed strange to your ears; but if we be a little vigilant, and accustom ourselves to them, we shall easily be able to teach others.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Meanwhile, it is necessary farther to say this to your Charity.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Even as God hath given us glory by means of this great creation, so let us also glorify Him by a pure conversation!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: “The heavens declare the glory of God,” though only seen; and we therefore should declare God’s glory not only in speaking, but in silence, and in astonishing all men by the brightness of our life.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For He saith, “Let your light so shine before men, that they may see your good works, and glorify your Father which is in heaven.”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For when an unbeliever beholds thee, who art a believer, subdued, modest, and orderly in manners, he will wonder and say, “Truly great is the God of the Christians!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: What manner of men hath He formed?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: What, and from what hath He made them?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Hath He turned them from men into angels?

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: If any one treats them contemptuously, they revile not!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: If any one beats them, they are not enraged!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: If any one does them an injury, they pray for him who has put them in pain!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: They have no enemy!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: They know nothing of cherishing malice!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: They are guiltless of vain babbling!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: They have not learnt to utter a falsehood!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: They cannot endure a false oath, or rather, they swear not at all, but would prefer to have their tongue cut out, rather than to let an oath proceed out of their mouth!”

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Such are the things which we should give them cause to say of us; and we should exterminate our evil habit of oaths, and pay at least as much honour to God, as we do to our more valuable garments.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For how truly absurd is it, that when we have one garment better than the rest, we do not suffer ourselves to be continually wearing it; and yet everywhere we draggle about the name of God without concern, or ceremony!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Let us not, I earnestly pray and beseech you, let us not thus despise our own salvation; but the care which we have used respecting this precept from the beginning, let us carry on even to the end.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For I thus continually exhort you on the subject of oaths, not as though condemning you of listlessness, but inasmuch as I have seen that ye are for the most part reformed, I press you, and am urgent, that the whole work should be finished off, and come to its perfection.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Even so act the spectators of public games.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: They excite those who are near the prize, with the more vehemence.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Let us, then, by no means become weary; for we have nearly reached the completion of this amendment; and the difficulty was at the beginning.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But now that the greater part of the evil habit has been cut away, and less remains to correct, no labour is necessary, but we only need a moderate degree of watchfulness, and diligence for some short time, in order that we ourselves being amended, may also become instructors to others; and that we may behold the Holy Passover with much confidence, and that with much pleasure we may reap a double or treble measure of the customary gladness of the festival.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For not so much does it delight us to be delivered from the toil and fatigue of fasting, as to meet that holy season with an illustrious and well-earned crown; a crown indeed that is never to fade!

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But in order that the amendment may take place the more quickly, do this which I tell thee.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: Inscribe upon the wall of thy house, and upon the wall of thy heart, that “flying sickle;” and think that it is flying forth on occasion of the curse, and constantly remember it.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: And if thou observest another person swearing, restrain, forbid, and be careful for him, and be careful for thine own domestics.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: For if we would look to this, that we might not merely correct ourselves, but also bring others to the same point, we shall ourselves quickly arrive at the goal; since while we undertake to instruct others, we shall be ashamed and blush, should we in our own case seem to leave those things unperformed, which we enjoin upon them.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: There is no need to say more; for much has been already spoken on these matters; and these things are now said only by way of remembrance.

verses.book: The Homilies on the Statues to the People of Antioch.
verses.text: But may God, who is more sparing of our souls than we are, make us perfect in this, and every good work; that so having completed the whole fruit of righteousness, we may be found worthy of the kingdom of heaven, through the grace and lovingkindness of our Lord Jesus Christ, through Whom, and with Whom, to the Father, with the Holy Ghost, be glory, for ever and ever.
